ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Chicken Methi; closest defensible match is chicken curry (FOODON_03302757). Fenugreek-specific variant not represented in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as base anchor for curried boneless chicken preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Chicken Methi curry. A00GJ (cooked chicken meat) is the closest defensible anchor. Gravy and spice matrix not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept maps specifically to Chicken Methi curry as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Chicken Methi is approximately 300 g, providing roughly 550 kcal, 35 g protein, 40 g fat, and 15 g carbohydrate per serving.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Chicken curry (boneless, spiced gravy) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Chicken, broiler, meat only, cooked, stewed (NDB 05064)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN provides the closest published reference for Indian chicken curry with spiced gravy. Per-100g values from the input (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against ICMR-NIN chicken curry data and USDA SR Legacy stewed chicken. Energy 183 kcal, protein 11.7 g, fat 13.3 g, carbohydrate 5.0 g are consistent with a mixed chicken-and-gravy preparation at approximately 50% chicken by weight. Sodium 266.7 mg/100g is plausible for a home-style Indian curry. Fenugreek leaf contribution to fibre (1.0 g/100g) and vitamin C (3.3 mg/100g) is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for methi leaves (FDC ID 169247). Values retained with medium confidence given ai_generated provenance.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283 — analogous estimate based on mixed meat curry with low-carbohydrate spiced gravy
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Methi in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on: (1) the dish is predominantly protein and fat with only 5 g carbohydrate/100g; (2) analogous mixed meat curries with spiced tomato-onion gravy are classified low-GI in the International Tables (Atkinson et al. 2008); (3) fenugreek seeds and leaves have documented hypoglycaemic and GI-lowering properties (Neelakantan N et al., Nutrients 2014). Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (35 × 15 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 5.25 ÷ 2 ≈ 2.6 (rounded), classified very low.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 266.7 mg/100g; 800 mg per 300 g serving approaches 35% of the 2300 mg daily limit
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion to 150–200 g; pairing with potassium-rich vegetables (e.g., spinach, sweet potato) can partially offset the sodium load from this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (5 g/100g) and evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties of fenugreek (methi) leaves support glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken Methi is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in a standard 300 g portion paired with a low-GI carbohydrate such as brown rice or whole-wheat roti; the high saturated fat content (6.7 g/100g) should be moderated by requesting yogurt-based rather than cream-based preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.67 g/100g (20 g per 300 g serving) and trans fat 0.17 g/100g are clinically relevant for cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150 g and request preparation with minimal oil and no cream, substituting low-fat yogurt to reduce saturated fat intake below the 10 g per meal threshold recommended by the American Heart Association.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.67 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold; tomatoes and green chillies are nightshade ingredients that may trigger individual flares
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute cream with low-fat yogurt to reduce saturated fat below 5 g/100g, avoid or reduce nightshade ingredients (tomatoes, green chillies), and retain anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, fenugreek) which provide meaningful polyphenol benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Methi (also known as Methi Chicken or Murgh Methi) is a popular North Indian curry dish consisting of boneless chicken pieces slow-cooked in a rich, aromatic spiced gravy enriched with fresh fenugreek leaves (methi). The dish is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore and is classified under the Meat and Alternatives food group, subgroup Curried Meat. Preparation involves sautéing onions, garlic, and ginger in oil until golden, adding tomatoes and whole spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala), then simmering boneless chicken pieces until tender before folding in fresh or dried fenugreek leaves. The result is a semi-dry to moderately gravied curry with a slightly bitter, herbaceous note from the methi balanced by the warmth of the spice blend. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 183 kcal, 11.7 g protein, 13.3 g fat (of which 6.7 g is saturated), and only 5 g carbohydrate, making it a low-carbohydrate, protein-moderate dish. Sodium is 266.7 mg/100g, which is borderline for hypertension management. Fenugreek leaves contribute dietary fibre (1.0 g/100g), iron (1.0 mg/100g), calcium (50 mg/100g), and vitamin C (3.3 mg/100g), and have well-documented hypoglycaemic properties relevant to Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is halal and gluten-free. It is typically served with naan, roti, or rice and is suitable for lunch or dinner. Clinically, the saturated fat content is a concern for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis patients, and sodium management is important for hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 35 (low) based on its low carbohydrate content and the GI-lowering effect of fenugreek.
common_user_queries: Is chicken methi good for diabetes?
What are fenugreek leaves good for?

confidence_reason: Input values are ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from a delivery platform source (GrabFood), which is a forbidden primary nutrient source. Values have been cross-checked against ICMR-NIN Indian chicken curry data and USDA SR Legacy stewed chicken and are broadly consistent. Medium confidence is assigned because no exact approved-source match for Chicken Methi exists; values are adapted from the closest defensible analogues. GI is an analogous estimate.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Chicken Methi curry is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish; only total fat and saturated fat are reported. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this specific preparation is not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy for the composite dish; estimated ~320 mg/100g from ingredient analysis but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore not entered as a positive value. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values for the composite Chicken Methi dish are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 4. trans_fat_g 0.17 g/100g is retained from input; this is plausible for a preparation using commercial cooking oil and is consistent with USDA data for cooked chicken dishes. 5. The original input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not correspond to the HPB Foodo nutrient database and was not used as a primary nutrient source; ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy were used instead. 6. Two portions in the input both list 300 g as default; one default portion (1 bowl, 300 g) is retained. 7. No zero-macro justification is required as protein_g (11.67), fat_g (13.33), and carbohydrate_g (5.0) are all non-zero. 8. Q8 alias self-check (iteration 3): all strings in aliases_by_language verified verbatim against flat aliases array — english: [Chicken Methi ✓, Methi Chicken ✓, Fenugreek Chicken Curry ✓, Chicken Fenugreek ✓, Chicken with Fenugreek Leaves ✓, Indian Fenugreek Chicken ✓]; chinese: [葫芦巴鸡肉咖喱 ✓, 葫蘆巴雞肉咖喱 ✓]; malay: [Kari Ayam Methi ✓]; tamil: [வெந்தயம் கோழி கறி ✓]; hindi: [मेथी चिकन ✓, मुर्ग मेथी ✓]; romanized: [Methi Chicken ✓, Murgh Methi ✓, Methi Murgh ✓]. All 14 aliases_by_language strings confirmed present in flat aliases array.
